questions.questionText: Pharmacodynamics is defined as the study of:
questions.optionB: What the body does to the drug
questions.optionC: What the drug does to the body
questions.explanation: Pharmacokinetics is what the body does to the drug (ADME).

questions.questionText: The bioavailability of a drug administered intravenously is by definition:
questions.explanation: IV drugs enter the systemic circulation directly, bypassing absorption barriers.

questions.questionText: The therapeutic index (TI) of a drug is a measure of its:
questions.explanation: TI is the ratio of Toxic Dose (TD50) to Effective Dose (ED50). Higher TI = safer drug.

questions.questionText: First-pass metabolism primarily occurs in the:
questions.explanation: Orally administered drugs pass through the portal circulation and liver before reaching systemic circulation.

questions.questionText: Which of the following describes a 'Prodrug'?
questions.optionA: A drug that produces only side effects
questions.optionB: A drug that inhibits its own metabolism
questions.optionC: A fully active compound that is not metabolized
questions.optionD: An inactive precursor rapidly converted to the active form in the body
questions.explanation: E.g., Enalapril is converted to the active enalaprilat in the liver.

questions.questionText: A competitive antagonist affects the dose-response curve of an agonist by:
questions.optionB: Shifting it to the right with no change in maximal effect (Emax)
questions.optionD: Shifting it to the right and decreasing the maximal effect
questions.explanation: High doses of agonist can overcome competitive antagonism, preserving Vmax/Emax.

questions.questionText: The half-life (t1/2) of a drug exhibiting first-order kinetics is:
questions.optionA: Inversely proportional to the volume of distribution
questions.optionB: Directly proportional to the drug concentration
questions.optionC: Constant and independent of the drug concentration
questions.explanation: First-order kinetics eliminate a constant fraction (percentage) per unit time.

questions.questionText: Cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes are primarily involved in which phase of drug metabolism?
questions.explanation: CYP enzymes introduce or unmask polar functional groups.

questions.questionText: Glucuronidation is the most common reaction in which phase of drug metabolism?
questions.explanation: Phase II synthetic reactions conjugate the drug to a large, polar molecule for easier excretion.

questions.questionText: Tolerance to a drug means that:
questions.optionA: The drug causes opposite effects to those intended
questions.optionB: The drug is rapidly metabolized on the first pass
questions.optionC: The patient developed an allergic reaction
questions.optionD: Higher doses are required to produce the same pharmacological effect over time
questions.explanation: Receptor downregulation or increased metabolism often mediates tolerance.

questions.questionText: Which receptor type mediates the slowing of the heart rate by the vagus nerve?
questions.explanation: M2 receptors are abundant in the SA and AV nodes. Activation by Acetylcholine decreases HR.

questions.questionText: The primary neurotransmitter released by postganglionic sympathetic neurons to most target organs is:
questions.explanation: Exception: Sweat glands, which are sympathetically innervated but use acetylcholine.

questions.questionText: Atropine is a competitive antagonist at which type of receptor?
questions.explanation: Atropine blocks parasympathetic activity (e.g., dilates pupils, dry mouth, tachy).

questions.questionText: Which pure beta-1 selective blocker is most appropriate for a patient with asthma and angina?
questions.explanation: Cardioselective beta-1 blockers avoid bronchoconstriction mediated by beta-2 block.

questions.questionText: Prazosin lowers blood pressure primarily by blocking:
questions.optionA: Alpha-1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle
questions.explanation: Prazosin is a selective competitive alpha-1 antagonist, causing vasodilation.

questions.questionText: Activation of Beta-2 adrenergic receptors typically causes:
questions.optionA: Bronchodilation and vasodilation of skeletal muscle beds
questions.explanation: Beta-2 agonists (e.g., Albuterol) are used largely for asthma relief.

questions.questionText: Physostigmine is an indirect-acting cholinomimetic that works by:
questions.explanation: This increases synaptic acetylcholine levels. It crosses the BBB to treat atropine poisoning.

questions.questionText: An organophosphate poisoning patient is treated with Atropine and Pralidoxime (PAM). PAM works by:
questions.optionA: Directly degrading the organophosphate in the blood
questions.optionB: Acting as a central muscarinic antagonist
questions.explanation: PAM binds the phosphorylated enzyme and removes the phosphate group.

questions.questionText: Which sympathomimetic amine is the drug of choice for anaphylactic shock?
questions.explanation: Epinephrine strongly activates alpha-1 (vasoconstriction) and beta-2 (bronchodilation) to reverse shock.

questions.questionText: Clonidine acts primarily as a(n):
questions.explanation: Stimulation of central presynaptic alpha-2 receptors decreases sympathetic outflow, lowering BP.

questions.questionText: The primary mechanism of action of Thiazide diuretics (e.g., Hydrochlorothiazide) is:
questions.optionA: Aldosterone antagonism in the collecting duct
questions.optionB: Inhibition of the Na+/Cl- cotransporter in the distal convoluted tubule
questions.optionC: Carbonic anhydrase inhibition in the proximal tubule
questions.optionD: Inhibition of the Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter in the Loop of Henle
questions.explanation: They are first-line for uncomplicated essential hypertension.

questions.questionText: Which diuretic is most appropriate for rapid relief of acute pulmonary edema in heart failure?
questions.explanation: Loop diuretics have profound and rapid diuretic effects ('high ceiling').

questions.questionText: ACE inhibitors (e.g., Lisinopril) lower blood pressure and predictably cause which common side effect due to bradykinin accumulation?
questions.explanation: ACE degrades bradykinin; its inhibition leads to bradykinin accumulation in the lungs.

questions.questionText: Calcium channel blockers (e.g., Amlodipine, Verapamil) primarily block which type of calcium channel?
questions.explanation: They relax vascular smooth muscle (dihydropyridines) and slow cardiac conduction (non-dihydropyridines).

questions.questionText: Digoxin increases myocardial contractility (positive inotropy) by inhibiting the:
questions.explanation: Inhibition raises intracellular Na+, which reverses the Na+/Ca2+ exchanger, increasing intracellular Ca2+.

questions.questionText: Nitroglycerin relieves angina primarily by:
questions.optionB: Directly dilating severely atherosclerotic coronary arteries
questions.optionC: Releasing Nitric Oxide (NO) which causes profound venodilation and reduces preload
questions.optionD: Blocking beta-1 receptors to slow the heart rate
questions.explanation: Venodilation pools blood, decreasing returning volume (preload) and myocardial oxygen demand.

questions.questionText: Which class of antiarrhythmic drugs primarily blocks fast sodium channels (Phase 0)?
questions.explanation: Class I agents define their action by slowing the upstroke of the action potential.

questions.questionText: Amiodarone is a highly effective Class III antiarrhythmic but has significant long-term side effects including:
questions.optionD: Pulmonary fibrosis, thyroid dysfunction, and corneal microdeposits
questions.explanation: Amiodarone contains iodine (thyroid effects) and is highly lipophilic with long half-life tissue accumulation.

questions.questionText: Statins (e.g., Atorvastatin) lower serum cholesterol primarily by inhibiting:
questions.explanation: By inhibiting endogenous cholesterol synthesis in the liver, they upregulate LDL receptors.

questions.questionText: Which lipid-lowering agent powerfully reduces triglycerides and can cause flushing and pruritus (preventable with aspirin)?
questions.explanation: Niacin inhibits lipolysis in adipose tissue and reduces VLDL synthesis in the liver.

questions.questionText: The mechanism of action of Penicillins and Cephalosporins involves:
questions.optionD: Inhibition of bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to Penicillin-Binding Proteins (PBPs)
questions.explanation: Beta-lactams are bactericidal, disrupting the cross-linking of peptidoglycan.

questions.questionText: Which class of antibiotics inhibits protein synthesis by binding to the 50S ribosomal subunit?
questions.explanation: Macrolides, Clindamycin, and Linezolid bind 50S (buy AT 30, CCEL at 50).

questions.questionText: Aminoglycosides (e.g., Gentamicin) exhibit notable serious toxicities affecting the:
questions.explanation: Irreversible hearing loss and acute tubular necrosis are major risks.

questions.questionText: Which antibiotic classically causes 'Red Man Syndrome' (histamine release) when infused intravenously too rapidly?
questions.explanation: Vancomycin requires slow infusion to prevent this non-allergic histamine release.

questions.questionText: Fluoroquinolones (e.g., Ciprofloxacin) exert their bactericidal effect by inhibiting:
questions.optionB: DNA Gyrase (Topoisomerase II) and Topoisomerase IV
questions.explanation: This prevents DNA supercoiling and uncoiling necessary for replication.

questions.questionText: The combination of Sulfamethoxazole and Trimethoprim (Cotrimoxazole) is deeply synergistic because they:
questions.optionA: Prevent protein synthesis at 30S and 50S subunits
questions.optionB: Increase one another's renal excretion half-life
questions.optionC: Both destroy the cell wall simultaneously
questions.optionD: Sequentially block sequential steps in bacterial folic acid synthesis
questions.explanation: Sulfa drugs inhibit dihydropteroate synthase; Trimethoprim inhibits dihydrofolate reductase.

questions.questionText: Isoniazid (INH), a first-line antitubercular drug, requires co-administration of which vitamin to prevent peripheral neuropathy?
questions.explanation: INH competitively inhibits Vitamin B6 utilization.

questions.questionText: Amphotericin B, a potent systemic antifungal, binds to the fungal cell membrane by interacting with:
questions.explanation: This forms artificial pores that cause lethal leakage of intracellular ions.

questions.questionText: Acyclovir is a guanosine analog specifically useful against Herpes Simplex Virus because it requires activation by the viral enzyme:
questions.explanation: Only infected cells with viral thymidine kinase can efficiently phosphorylate and trap acyclovir.

questions.questionText: Methotrexate acts as a chemotherapeutic agent by competitively inhibiting:
questions.explanation: This deprives the rapidly dividing cell of tetrahydrofolate needed for purine/thymidine synthesis.
